summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Charles Petzold's 'The Annotated Turing,' a book that guides readers through Alan Turing's seminal 1936 paper on computability. He emphasizes that Turing's thirty-six page paper established the mathematical foundation for all modern computers. Beyond computing, Atwood highlights Turing's contributions to breaking the Enigma code and founding artificial intelligence concepts. The post takes a somber turn addressing Turing's persecution for homosexuality and his tragic suicide at age 42. Atwood celebrates both Petzold's nine-year effort and Turing's enduring legacy through the Turing Award and memorial.
key_points: Every modern computer is based on the mathematical model from Turing's 1936 paper, just thirty-six pages long
Turing made three foundational contributions: computability theory, breaking Enigma in WWII, and proposing the Turing Test for AI
Turing was criminally prosecuted for homosexuality in 1952 and subjected to degrading treatment before his suicide at 42
The Turing Memorial statue depicts him holding an apple, symbolizing both his death by cyanide-laced apple and referencing Newton and forbidden love
Petzold's annotated guide took nine years to write and makes Turing's dense mathematical work accessible
money_quotes: The computer you're using to read this post is based on the mathematical model laid out in that thirty-six page 1936 paper. As are all other computers in the world.
Not only is Alan Turing the father of all modern computer science, he also was the single individual most responsible for breaking the Enigma code during World War II.
It pains me greatly to read about the degrading and inhumane treatment one of our greatest scientific minds was subjected to.
Reading the list of Turing Award recipients is humbling indeed, a reminder of not only how far we've come, but how far we have to go.
Mathematics, rightly viewed, possesses not only truth, but supreme beauty – a beauty cold and austere, like that of sculpture.
key_insight: Alan Turing's thirty-six page 1936 paper created the mathematical foundation for all modern computing, yet the man who gave us so much was tragically destroyed by society's persecution of his identity.
